chapters.title: Home
chapters.content:
# CAIR Coding Guide

A practical engineering reference for all software contributors at the **Centre for Artificial Intelligence Research (CAIR)**, Memorial University of Newfoundland.

---

## What Is This Guide?

This guide establishes consistent coding standards across CAIR projects — from research prototypes to production infrastructure. It covers naming conventions, code style, security, documentation, performance, and more.

Whether you are onboarding or reviewing a pull request, this guide is your reference.

---

## How to Use It

- **New contributors** — read chapters 1–3 before writing code.
- **Code reviewers** — use the [Code Review Guidelines](#code-review) as a checklist.
- **Everyone** — search with the box in the sidebar or click any chapter to jump directly to it.

---

## Chapters at a Glance

| # | Chapter | What you will find |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | [Introduction](#introduction) | Purpose, audience, and benefits of this guide |
| 2 | [Core Coding Principles](#core-coding-principles) | DRY, KISS, YAGNI, SOLID |
| 3 | [Code Style & Formatting](#code-style-and-formatting) | Language-specific rules and formatters |
| 4 | [Variable Naming](#variable-naming) | Naming by scope, type, and language |
| 5 | [Functions & Methods](#functions-and-methods) | Single responsibility, naming, params |
| 6 | [Control Structures](#control-structures) | If/else, loops, guard clauses |
| 7 | [OOP & Design](#oop) | Classes, encapsulation, design patterns |
| 8 | [Error Handling & Logging](#error-handling) | Exceptions, log levels, structured logs |
| 9 | [Security](#security) | Input validation, secrets, OWASP Top 10 |
| 10 | [Performance](#performance) | Profiling, algorithms, caching |
| 11 | [Infrastructure](#infrastructure) | Docker, containers, VMs |
| 12 | [Documentation](#documentation) | Comments, docstrings, changelogs |
| 13 | [API Documentation](#api-documentation) | OpenAPI, versioning, examples |
| 14 | [Code Review](#code-review) | Process, checklist, feedback norms |
| 15 | [Appendix](#appendix) | References, glossary, tools |

---

> This is a living document. If you spot an error or want to propose an update, open a pull request on [GitHub](https://github.com/CAIR-MUN/CAIR-CodeGuide).

chapters.title: Introduction
chapters.content:
Purpose of the Coding Guide

This Coding Guide is designed to establish a clear, consistent, and
practical framework for writing software code within our data center
projects. It aims to ensure that developers adhere to best practices
focusing on code quality, maintainability, security, and collaboration.
By following these standards, we can reduce errors, accelerate
onboarding, and facilitate smoother teamwork across all project phases.

Audience and Scope

This guide is intended for all software engineers, developers, and
technical contributors involved in the development, maintenance, and
review of code in our data center projects. It applies to all
programming languages and technologies used within the organization,
serving as a common reference to align coding efforts and expectations.
Whether you are a novice developer or a seasoned engineer, this guide
provides important principles and actionable standards for everyday
coding tasks.

How to Use This Guide

The guide is organized into clear, focused sections covering
foundational coding principles, naming conventions, function design,
security practices, documentation, testing, and more. Readers are
encouraged to apply the relevant sections to their work, consult it
during code reviews, and share it with peers to promote a unified coding
culture. The guide also accommodates language and project-specific
details where applicable and will be updated regularly to incorporate
new best practices and tool recommendations.

Benefits of Coding Standards

- Adherence to coding standards brings numerous benefits, including:

- Improved code readability which makes code easier to understand,
  review, and maintain.

- Consistency across projects and teams that reduces cognitive load and
  communication barriers.

- Enhanced code quality and reliability through emphasis on secure,
  reusable, and testable code.

- Faster onboarding of new team members by providing clear guidance and
  reducing knowledge silos.

- Minimized technical debt and bugs via disciplined design and
  documentation practices.

- Facilitated collaboration and knowledge sharing by creating a shared
  language and expectations around code.

This guide is a living document that will evolve with our projects and
technology landscape, supporting our mission to deliver robust and
secure software solutions efficiently.

How to Contribute

This guide is a collaborative effort, and we encourage all developers to
contribute to its improvement. If you have suggestions, corrections, or
ideas for new sections, please follow these steps:

1.  **Fork the repository** containing this guide.
2.  **Create a new branch** for your changes.
3.  **Make your changes** and submit a pull request.
4.  **Tag a senior developer** for review.

Your contributions will help us maintain a high-quality, relevant, and
practical coding guide for everyone.

chapters.title: Core Coding Principles
chapters.content:
## 2.1 DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)

The DRY principle states that "Every piece of knowledge must have a
single, unambiguous, authoritative representation within a system." In
practice, this means avoiding duplication of code, logic, or data. When
repeated code exists, making changes becomes error-prone and
time-consuming as developers must update each duplicate separately.
Adhering to DRY improves maintainability, reduces bugs, and promotes
code reuse.

### Importance

- Centralizes logic for easier updates and consistent behavior.

- Reduces risk of introducing inconsistent changes.

- Simplifies debugging and testing by limiting the number of code
  locations to verify.

### Examples to Avoid

• Copy-pasting validation logic across multiple functions.

• Repeating the same calculation or formatting code instead of creating
reusable functions.

• Duplicating configuration values or constants.

### Best Practices to Achieve DRY

• Use functions or methods for repeated logic.

• Create shared utility libraries or modules.

• Consolidate constants and configuration in one location.

• Apply inheritance or composition for similar class behaviors.

### Python Example

**Non-DRY Code:**
```python
def process_order(order):
    # 10 lines of validation logic
    if not order.get("customer_email"):
        raise ValueError("Customer email is required.")
    if not order.get("items"):
        raise ValueError("Order must have items.")
    # ... more validation ...

    print("Processing order...")

def process_refund(refund):
    # The same 10 lines of validation logic, copied and pasted
    if not refund.get("customer_email"):
        raise ValueError("Customer email is required.")
    if not refund.get("items"):
        raise ValueError("Refund must have items.")
    # ... more validation ...

    print("Processing refund...")

```

**DRY Code:**
```python
def validate_request(request):
    """A single function to validate common fields."""
    if not request.get("customer_email"):
        raise ValueError("Customer email is required.")
    if not request.get("items"):
        raise ValueError("Request must have items.")
    # ... more validation ...

def process_order(order):
    validate_request(order)
    print("Processing order...")

def process_refund(refund):
    validate_request(refund)
    print("Processing refund...")
```

## 2.2 KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid)

The KISS principle encourages writing code that is as simple as possible
without unnecessary complexity. Complex solutions are harder to
understand, maintain, and modify. Strive for clarity, straightforward
logic, and avoid over-engineering.

### Simplification Strategies

• Break problems into smaller, manageable functions.

• Avoid deep nesting of conditional statements.

• Use descriptive variable and function names.

• Prefer readable code over clever or overly concise constructions.

### Python Example

**Complex Code (non-KISS):**
```python
def get_user_status(user):
    # Obscure one-liner using nested ternary operators
    return "active" if user.is_active else "inactive" if user.last_login < thirty_days_ago else "archived"
```

**Simple Code (KISS):**
```python
def get_user_status(user):
    """Clear and readable logic for determining user status."""
    if not user.is_active:
        return "inactive"
    if user.last_login < thirty_days_ago:
        return "archived"
    return "active"
```

### Anti-patterns to Avoid

• Premature optimization causing convoluted code.

• Overuse of design patterns where plain code suffices.

• Excessive abstraction leading to difficult-to-trace code.

## 2.3 YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)

YAGNI warns against adding functionality until it is necessary.
Overbuilding features or generalizing code for hypothetical future needs
leads to wasted effort and unnecessarily complicated codebases.

### Guidance

• Implement only the required features at present.

• Refactor and extend code when actual needs arise.

• Avoid speculative abstractions or optimizations.

### Python Example

**YAGNI Violation:**
A developer is asked to build a function to export user data to CSV. They speculate that in the future, they might need to export to JSON, XML, and other formats, so they build a complex, generic export system.

```python
class Exporter:
    def __init__(self, format="csv", **kwargs):
        self.format = format
        # ... complex setup for different formatters ...

    def export(self, data):
        if self.format == "csv":
            # ... CSV export logic ...
        elif self.format == "json":
            # ... JSON export logic (not needed yet) ...
        elif self.format == "xml":
            # ... XML export logic (not needed yet) ...
        else:
            raise ValueError(f"Unsupported format: {self.format}")
```
**YAGNI-compliant:**
Implement only the CSV export functionality that is currently required. If other formats are needed later, the function can be refactored then.
```python
def export_users_to_csv(users):
    """Exports a list of users to a CSV file."""
    # ... simple, direct CSV export logic ...
```

## 2.4 SOLID Principles (Overview)

The SOLID principles are a set of object-oriented design guidelines that
promote maintainable, scalable, and robust software.

• S: Single Responsibility Principle: A class/function should have one
and only one reason to change.

• O: Open/Closed Principle: Software entities should be open for
extension, but closed for modification.

• L: Liskov Substitution Principle: Objects should be replaceable by
instances of their subtypes without altering correctness.

• I: Interface Segregation Principle: Many client-specific interfaces
are better than one general-purpose interface.

• D: Dependency Inversion Principle: Depend on abstractions, not on
concrete implementations.

These principles help manage complexity and improve code flexibility,
particularly for large codebases.

## 2.5 OWASP Secure Coding Principles

Security must be integral to coding practices to protect systems and
data from vulnerabilities. The OWASP (Open Web Application Security
Project) provides guidelines and best practices to guide secure
development.

### Security Mindset

• Treat all input as untrusted and validate it rigorously.

• Prefer secure defaults and enforce the principle of least privilege.

• Avoid exposing sensitive information in logs, error messages, or
source code.

• Keep dependencies up-to-date to mitigate known vulnerabilities.

### Common Threat Examples

• Injection Attacks: SQL, command injection by unsanitized inputs.

• Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): Malicious scripts injected into web pages.

• Broken Authentication: Weak login, session management.

• Sensitive Data Exposure: Inadequate encryption or data masking.

Adopting these secure coding principles minimizes the attack surface and
helps build resilient, trusted applications.

chapters.title: Code Style and Formatting
chapters.content:
# 3. Code Style and Formatting

Consistent code style and formatting are essential for readability, maintainability, and collaboration. This guide outlines the principles and tools we use to maintain a high standard of code quality.

## 3.1 Guiding Principles

-   **Readability:** Code is read more often than it is written. Prioritize clarity.
-   **Consistency:** A consistent style allows developers to focus on the logic, not the formatting.
-   **Automation:** Use automated tools to format and lint code. This saves time and prevents debates about style.

## 3.2 Language-Specific Guidelines

### 3.2.1 Python

-   **Style Guide:** We follow **PEP 8**, the official style guide for Python code.
-   **Formatter:** We use **`black`** for uncompromising code formatting. It is not configurable, which guarantees consistency.
-   **Linter:** We use **`ruff`** (or `flake8`) to check for errors and style issues. `ruff` is extremely fast and can also be used for formatting.

**Example Configuration (`pyproject.toml` for `ruff`):**
```toml
[tool.ruff]
line-length = 88
select = ["E", "F", "W", "I"] # E/F/W are pyflakes/pycodestyle, I is isort
```

### 3.2.2 JavaScript / TypeScript

-   **Formatter:** We use **`Prettier`** to ensure a consistent code style.
-   **Linter:** We use **`ESLint`** to find and fix problems in JavaScript code.

**Example Configuration (`.prettierrc.json`):**
```json
{
  "semi": true,
  "singleQuote": true,
  "trailingComma": "es5"
}
```

**Example Configuration (`.eslintrc.json`):**
```json
{
  "extends": [
    "eslint:recommended",
    "plugin:prettier/recommended"
  ],
  "rules": {
    "no-console": "warn"
  }
}
```

### 3.2.3 Shell Scripting

For shell scripts, we use **`ShellCheck`** to identify common issues and potential bugs.

-   Always run `shellcheck` on your scripts before committing them.
-   Use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` as the shebang.
-   Use `set -euo pipefail` to make your scripts more robust.

## 3.3 Automated Tooling

We use automated tools to enforce our style guides. This is not optional.

### 3.3.1 Pre-commit Hooks

We use **`pre-commit`** to run formatters and linters before code is committed. This ensures that only clean, well-formatted code enters our repository.

**Example `.pre-commit-config.yaml`:**
```yaml
repos:
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.3.0
    hooks:
    -   id: trailing-whitespace
    -   id: end-of-file-fixer
    -   id: check-yaml
-   repo: https://github.com/psf/black
    rev: 22.6.0
    hooks:
    -   id: black
-   repo: https://github.com/charliermarsh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: 'v0.0.275'
    hooks:
    -   id: ruff
-   repo: https://github.com/prettier/prettier
    rev: '2.8.0'
    hooks:
    -   id: prettier
-   repo: https://github.com/koalaman/shellcheck-precommit
    rev: v0.8.0
    hooks:
    -   id: shellcheck
```
To use this, install `pre-commit` and run `pre-commit install`.

### 3.3.2 CI/CD Integration

All formatters and linters should also be run in the CI/CD pipeline. This provides a final check and ensures that all code in the repository adheres to our standards.

**Example CI step (GitHub Actions):**
```yaml
- name: Run Linters
  run: |
    pip install pre-commit
    pre-commit run --all-files
```

## 3.4 General Formatting Rules

-   **Indentation:** Use spaces, not tabs. 4 spaces for Python, 2 for JavaScript/TypeScript.
-   **Line Length:** Keep lines to a reasonable length (e.g., 88 characters for Python, 80 for Prettier defaults).
-   **Whitespace:** Use whitespace to improve readability, but avoid trailing whitespace.
-   **Comments:** Comment the *why*, not the *what*. Keep comments up to date.
-   **Naming Conventions:** Follow the standard naming conventions for the language you are using (e.g., `snake_case` in Python, `camelCase` in JavaScript).

chapters.title: Variable Naming Conventions
chapters.content:
Consistent and meaningful variable names are essential for readability,
maintainability, and effective communication in code. This chapter
outlines best practices and conventions for naming variables based on
type, scope, and case style with examples suitable for languages used in
data centers such as Python, Java, C++, and JavaScript.

## 4.1 Guidelines per Data Type or Scope

• Descriptive and specific: Variable names should clearly describe their
purpose or the data they hold. For example, use `userCount` instead of
generic names like `count` or `data`.

• Boolean variables: Start with prefixes like `is`, `has`, `can`, or
`should`, e.g., `isConnected`, `hasAccess`, to imply true/false values
clearly.

• Collections/arrays: Use plural nouns like `userList` or `fileNames` for
arrays or lists.

• Temporary or loop variables: Single-letter names such as `i`, `j`, or
`k` are acceptable in small, localized scopes like loops.

## 4.2 Global, Local, Constants Naming

• Global variables: Should be used sparingly. Use longer, descriptive
names with clear prefixes if needed to avoid conflicts, e.g.,
`globalTimeoutMilliseconds`.

• Local variables: Can be more concise but still meaningful, e.g.,
`tempResult` or `index`.

• Constants: Should be written in uppercase with underscores separating
words (screaming snake case), e.g., `MAX_RETRIES`, `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT`.
This visually distinguishes them from variables that can change.

## 4.3 Meaningful, Readable Names

• Choose whole words over abbreviations unless very common (e.g., `num`
for number is acceptable, but `usr` for user is not recommended).

• Avoid cryptic names that make it hard to understand the variable's
purpose without additional context.

• If a variable represents a specific concept, its name should reflect
it precisely, e.g., `invoiceTotalAmount` instead of `amount`.

## 4.4 Avoid Abbreviations and Unclear Acronyms

• Abbreviations should be avoided unless they are widely understood and
accepted in the domain (e.g., HTTP, URL).

• If abbreviating, be consistent throughout the codebase. Avoid mixing
`num`, `cnt`, and `number` for the same concept.

• Avoid acronyms that are not common knowledge to prevent confusion.

## 4.5 Examples of Good vs. Bad Names

| Bad Name | Reason | Good Name | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| a | Too vague, no meaning | userAge | Clear description of content |
| data | Too generic | fileList | Specific data type or collection |
| isReadyFlag | Redundant suffix | isReady | Boolean indicator without redundancy |
| tmp | Temporary but unclear | tempFilePath | Clarifies purpose and content |
| usrNm | Unclear abbreviation | userName | Clear, full words |

## 4.6 Case Style Rules

| Case Style | Usage | Example | Common Languages |
|---|---|---|---|
| camelCase | Variables, functions | userAge, fetchData | Java, JavaScript, C++ |
| PascalCase | Classes, types | UserAccount, Order | Java, C++, C# |
| snake_case | Variables, functions | user_age, fetch_data | Python, Ruby |
| UPPER_SNAKE_CASE | Constants | MAX_RETRIES, API_KEY | All (common practice) |

## 4.7 Language-specific examples

Python:
```python
# snake_case for variables and functions
user_age = 42
def fetch_data(file_path):
	return open(file_path).read()
# constants
MAX_RETRIES = 5
```

JavaScript:
```javascript
// camelCase for variables and functions
let userAge = 42;
function fetchData(filePath) {
  return fetch(filePath);
}
// constants (often UPPER_SNAKE_CASE)
const MAX_RETRIES = 5;
```

Java:
```java
// camelCase for variables and methods, PascalCase for classes
public class UserAccount {
	private int userAge;
	public int getUserAge() { return userAge; }
	public static final int MAX_RETRIES = 5;
}
```

C++:
```cpp
// camelCase or snake_case depends on project; classes often PascalCase
class UserAccount {
public:
	int userAge;
	static constexpr int MAX_RETRIES = 5;
};
```

Consistently applying these naming conventions ensures code is easier to
read, maintain, and collaborate on, reducing cognitive barriers for all
team members.

chapters.title: Functions and Methods
chapters.content:
Functions and methods are fundamental building blocks of software
design. Writing clear, focused, and maintainable functions is crucial
for building reliable and understandable data center software projects.
This chapter outlines best practices to design functions with single
responsibility, clear naming, proper parameter usage, robust error
handling, and correct visibility.

## 5.1 Single Responsibility and Cohesion

Each function or method should have a **single responsibility** --- it
should do one thing and do it well. This concept, part of the Single
Responsibility Principle (SRP), promotes highly cohesive functions where
the logic inside relates to one specific task or purpose.

### Benefits of Single Responsibility:

- Simplifies understanding and debugging.

- Enhances reuse and testing since functions are isolated and
  predictable.

- Reduces risk of unintended side effects from overlapping
  responsibilities.

### Example

Bad:

```python
def process_user(user_data):
    # Validates user data, saves to database, and sends welcome email
    validate(user_data)
    save_to_db(user_data)
    send_email(user_data['email'])
```

Good:

```python
def validate_user(user_data):
    # Validate user input data
    pass

def save_user(user_data):
    # Save user to database
    pass

def send_welcome_email(email):
    # Send welcome email
    pass
```

## 5.2 Naming Functions Clearly

Function names should clearly describe **what** the function does,
preferably with a verb or verb phrase. Avoid vague or overloaded names.

### Guidelines

- Use action verbs: `calculateTotal()`, `fetchReport()`, `validateInput()`.

- Avoid generic names like `handleData()` or `processInfo()`.

- Follow consistent casing style per language conventions (camelCase,
snake_case, PascalCase).

## 5.3 Parameter and Return Value Conventions

- Keep the **number of parameters minimal**; consider grouping related
  parameters into objects or structures.

- Parameters should be named clearly to indicate their role.

- Document expected types, allowed values, and any side effects.

- Functions should have a **single return value** where possible. If
  multiple values are needed, use tuples, objects, or data structures.

### Example (Python)

```python
def calculate_area(width: float, height: float) -> float:
    return width * height
```

## 5.4 Error Handling Strategies

Robust functions anticipate and handle errors gracefully to maintain
system stability and provide useful feedback.

### Common practices:

- Use **exceptions** to handle unexpected or erroneous conditions
  (Java, Python, C++).

- Return special status codes or results indicating success or failure.

- Validate inputs early and raise errors or reject invalid data.

- Log errors at appropriate levels with contextual information.

### Example (Python):

```python
def open_file(file_path):
    try:
        with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
            return f.read()
    except FileNotFoundError:
        print("Error: File not found.")
        return None
```

## 5.5 Example Function Signatures and Formats

| Language | Function Signature Example |
|---|---|
| Python | `def fetch_user(user_id: int) -> dict:` |
| Java | `public User fetchUser(int userId) throws UserNotFoundException { ... }` |
| C++ | `User fetchUser(int userId);` |
| JavaScript | `function fetchUser(userId) { ... }` |

## 5.6 Visibility (Private, Public) and Accessibility Rules

Controlling function accessibility is important for encapsulation and
reducing unintended usage.

- **Public** functions form the interface with other components or
  external clients. They should be stable and well-documented.

- **Private/internal** functions support implementation details and
  should hide complexity. Naming or language-specific keywords often
  indicate private status (e.g., underscore prefix in Python `_helper_func()`,
  `private` keyword in Java/C++).

- Limit exposure of functions to only what is necessary for external use
  to reduce coupling and improve modularity.

chapters.title: Control Structures
chapters.content:
# 6. Control Structures

Control structures are the foundation of program flow, enabling decision-making, repetition, and branching. Using them effectively leads to readable, maintainable, and efficient code.

## 6.1 Conditional Statements

### 6.1.1 If-Else Statements

-   Keep conditions clear and concise. Extract complex conditions into well-named functions or variables.
-   Order your `if/elif/else` chain from most to least specific, or from most to least likely.

### 6.1.2 Switch Statements and Pattern Matching

For multi-way branching, `switch` statements or pattern matching are often cleaner than long `if/elif/else` chains.

**Python (3.10+) Pattern Matching:**
```python
def http_status(status):
    match status:
        case 200:
            return "OK"
        case 404:
            return "Not Found"
        case 500:
            return "Internal Server Error"
        case _:
            return "Unknown status"
```

## 6.2 Loops

### 6.2.1 General Best Practices

-   Prefer language-native iteration patterns (`for...in`, `for...of`) over manual index loops.
-   Keep loop bodies small. Extract complex logic into helper functions.

### 6.2.2 Language-Specific Idioms

**Python:**
-   **List Comprehensions:** A concise way to create lists.
    ```python
    squares = [x * x for x in range(10)]
    ```
-   **Generator Expressions:** Use these for large datasets to save memory.
    ```python
    squares_gen = (x * x for x in range(10))
    ```
-   **The `for...else` Block:** The `else` block executes only if the loop completes without hitting a `break` statement.
    ```python
    for i in range(5):
        if i == 5:
            print("Found 5!")
            break
    else:
        print("Did not find 5.") # This will be printed
    ```

**JavaScript:**
-   **`for...of` vs. `for...in`:**
    -   Use `for...of` to iterate over the values of an iterable (e.g., an array).
    -   Use `for...in` to iterate over the keys of an object.
-   **Functional Iteration (`map`, `filter`, `reduce`):** Prefer these methods for more declarative and readable code.
    ```javascript
    const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
    const evenSquares = numbers
        .filter(n => n % 2 === 0)
        .map(n => n * n); // [4, 16]
    ```

## 6.3 Early Exits and Guard Clauses

To reduce nesting and improve readability, use guard clauses and return early from functions.

**Before (deeply nested):**
```python
def process_user(user):
    if user:
        if user.is_active:
            if user.has_permissions:
                # ... do something
```

**After (with guard clauses):**
```python
def process_user(user):
    if not user:
        return
    if not user.is_active:
        return
    if not user.has_permissions:
        return
    # ... do something
```

## 6.4 Error Handling

Use control structures to handle errors gracefully.

### 6.4.1 `try...except` (Python) and `try...catch` (JavaScript)

-   **Be Specific:** Always catch specific exceptions, not generic ones (e.g., `except ValueError`, not `except Exception`).
-   **Use `finally`:** The `finally` block always executes, whether an exception occurred or not. It's useful for cleanup code (e.g., closing a file or a database connection).

**Python Example:**
```python
try:
    value = int(user_input)
except ValueError:
    print("Invalid input. Please enter a number.")
else:
    # The 'else' block runs if no exception was raised
    print(f"You entered {value}")
finally:
    # This block always runs
    print("Execution complete.")
```

**JavaScript Example:**
```javascript
try {
  const result = riskyOperation();
  console.log(result);
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof TypeError) {
    console.error("A type error occurred:", error.message);
  } else {
    console.error("An unknown error occurred:", error);
  }
} finally {
  console.log("Cleanup code runs here.");
}
```

chapters.title: Object-Oriented Design
Error Handling and Logging
chapters.content:
Effective error handling and logging are essential for building
resilient, observable, and maintainable systems. This chapter focuses on
practical patterns and examples developers should follow.

## 8.1 Exception Handling Conventions

- Fail fast: detect and raise errors as soon as a problem is discovered.

- Prefer specific exceptions over generic catches to avoid hiding bugs.

- Validate inputs early and raise clear exceptions with context.

- Use try/except or try/catch blocks only where you can handle or add
  meaningful context — otherwise allow errors to bubble to a higher
  level where they can be handled centrally.

- Avoid using exceptions for normal control flow.

- Clean up resources reliably using finally blocks, context managers
  (Python `with`), or language-specific constructs (e.g., try-with-
  resources in Java).

### Example (Python)

```python
def read_config(path):
    try:
        with open(path, 'r') as f:
            return f.read()
    except FileNotFoundError as e:
        raise ConfigurationError(f"Config not found: {path}") from e
```

## 8.2 Logging Levels and Message Standards

- DEBUG: Detailed technical information for debugging.
- INFO: High-level operational events (startup, shutdown, config).
- WARN: Recoverable problems or unexpected states.
- ERROR: Errors that prevent an operation from completing.
- FATAL/CRITICAL: Unrecoverable errors requiring immediate attention.

### Message content

- Include contextual keys (request id, user id, function name) to
  support log correlation.
- Avoid logging secrets or sensitive data.
- Prefer structured logs (JSON) for better parsing and observability.

## 8.3 Structured Logging Example

```json
{
  "timestamp": "2025-11-23T12:00:00Z",
  "level": "ERROR",
  "service": "ingest-service",
  "message": "Failed to process record",
  "record_id": "abc123",
  "error": "ValueError: invalid field"
}
```

## 8.4 Error Classification and Handling Patterns

- Transient vs Permanent errors: retry only transient errors with
  exponential backoff.
- Retry patterns: use idempotent operations when retrying.
- Circuit Breakers: prevent cascading failures by stopping attempts
  when a downstream system is unhealthy.

## 8.5 Observability and Monitoring

- Ensure errors increment counters/metrics and generate alerts for
  high-severity failure trends.
- Capture stack traces for ERROR-level logs to speed root-cause
  analysis.
- Use correlation IDs to trace requests end-to-end across services.

## 8.6 Practical Recommendations

- Centralize logging configuration and formats across services.
- Redact or mask sensitive fields before logging.
- Test error paths and log output as part of CI tests.

chapters.title: Security Best Practices
chapters.content:
# 9. Security Best Practices Security is a critical aspect of software development, especially in data center environments. This guide provides a comprehensive overview of security best practices that all developers should follow. ## 9.1 Secure Coding Practices ### 9.1.1 Input Validation Treat all input as untrusted. This includes user input, API requests, and data from other services. - **Be Specific:** Validate for type, length, format, and range. - **Use Whitelisting:** Only allow known-good values, rather than trying to block known-bad ones (blacklisting). - **Use Libraries:** Use well-tested validation libraries, such as `Pydantic` in Python or `Joi` in Node.js. **Python Example (Pydantic):** ```python from pydantic import BaseModel, constr, EmailStr class UserProfile(BaseModel): username: constr(min_length=3, max_length=50) email: EmailStr ``` ### 9.1.2 Output Encoding To prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), always encode data before displaying it in a web page. - **HTML Encoding:** Encode characters like `<` and `>` to `<` and `>`. - **Use Framework Features:** Modern web frameworks (e.g., React, Angular, Jinja2) automatically encode output. Rely on them. **Jinja2 Example:** ```html {# This is safe, as Jinja2 escapes the content by default #}

{{ user_provided_content }}

``` ### 9.1.3 Authentication and Password Management - **Use a Strong Hashing Algorithm:** Use Argon2 (preferred) or bcrypt to hash passwords. - **Never Store Passwords in Plain Text:** Or in a reversibly encrypted format. - **Implement Secure Session Management:** Generate a new session ID on login and invalidate it on logout. ### 9.1.4 Access Control Enforce the **Principle of Least Privilege**. Users and services should only have the permissions they absolutely need. **Python Example (Flask):** ```python from functools import wraps from flask import g, request, redirect, url_for def admin_required(f): @wraps(f) def decorated_function(*args, **kwargs): if not g.user.is_admin: return redirect(url_for('unauthorized')) return f(*args, **kwargs) return decorated_function @app.route('/admin') @admin_required def admin_panel(): return 'Admin Panel' ``` ## 9.2 Database Security ### 9.2.1 SQL Injection Prevention Always use parameterized queries (prepared statements) to interact with the database. Never use string formatting to build queries. **Python Example (SQLAlchemy):** ```python from sqlalchemy import text # Safe: uses bind parameters results = session.execute(text("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :user_id"), {"user_id": user_id}) ``` ### 9.2.2 Manage Database Permissions The application should connect to the database with a dedicated user that has the minimum required permissions. For example, a web application might only have `SELECT`, `INSERT`, and `UPDATE` permissions, but not `DROP` or `ALTER`. ### 9.2.3 Encrypt Data at Rest Sensitive data, such as Personally Identifiable Information (PII), should be encrypted in the database. Use either application-level encryption or the database's built-in Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) features. ## 9.3 API Security ### 9.3.1 Authentication and Authorization - **Use Standard Protocols:** Use OAuth 2.0 for authorization and OpenID Connect (OIDC) for authentication. - **Use JWTs for Stateless Authentication:** JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) are a good way to manage stateless authentication between services. ### 9.3.2 Rate Limiting Implement rate limiting to prevent abuse and Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks. **Example:** - Limit a user to 100 requests per minute. - Limit a more intensive endpoint to 10 requests per minute. ### 9.3.3 Secure Headers Use security-related HTTP headers to protect your application: - `Strict-Transport-Security`: Enforces the use of HTTPS. - `Content-Security-Policy`: Prevents XSS attacks. - `X-Content-Type-Options`: Prevents MIME-sniffing attacks. ### 9.3.4 Securely Handling URIs/URLs When building or handling URIs, it is important to prevent common vulnerabilities. - **Path Traversal:** If your application serves files based on a URL path, an attacker could use `../` to access files outside the intended directory. Always sanitize file paths and use a library to resolve them securely. - **Open Redirects:** If your application redirects to a URL provided by the user, an attacker could redirect users to a malicious site. Always validate that the redirect URL is on a trusted domain. **Python Example (preventing open redirects in Flask):** ```python from flask import request, redirect from urllib.parse import urlparse, urljoin def is_safe_url(target): ref_url = urlparse(request.host_url) test_url = urlparse(urljoin(request.host_url, target)) return test_url.scheme in ('http', 'https') and \ ref_url.netloc == test_url.netloc def redirect_back(default='home'): target = request.args.get('next') if target and is_safe_url(target): return redirect(target) return redirect(url_for(default)) ``` ## 9.4 Vulnerability Management ### 9.4.1 Dependency Scanning Use tools to scan your project's dependencies for known vulnerabilities. - **Python:** `pip-audit` or `safety` - **Node.js:** `npm audit` - **OWASP Dependency-Check:** A standalone tool that can scan for vulnerabilities in many languages. ### 9.4.2 SAST (Static Application Security Testing) Integrate SAST tools into your CI/CD pipeline to automatically scan your code for security vulnerabilities. - **SonarQube** - **GitHub CodeQL** ### 9.4.3 DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) Use DAST tools to test your running application for vulnerabilities. - **OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy)** - **Burp Suite** ## 9.5 Secrets Management Never hardcode secrets (API keys, database passwords, etc.) in your code. - **Environment Variables:** The simplest way to manage secrets. Use a `.env` file for local development. - **Vaults:** For production, use a dedicated secrets management tool like: - **HashiCorp Vault** - **AWS Secrets Manager** - **Google Cloud Secret Manager** ### 9.5.1 Storing and Managing `.env` Files `.env` files are used to store environment variables in a simple `KEY=VALUE` format. They are a convenient way to manage secrets and configuration for local development. **Key Best Practices:** 1. **NEVER commit `.env` files to version control.** They often contain sensitive credentials, and anyone with access to the repository would be able to see them. Add `.env` to your `.gitignore` file: ```gitignore # Ignore environment variables .env ``` 2. **Use a template file.** Create a file called `.env.example` or `.env.template` that includes all the necessary environment variables but with placeholder or empty values. This file *should* be committed to version control and serves as a template for other developers. **.env.example:** ``` DB_HOST=localhost DB_USER= DB_PASSWORD= API_KEY= ``` 3. **Load `.env` files in your application.** Use a library to load the variables from the `.env` file into the environment. **Python (with `python-dotenv`):** ```python from dotenv import load_dotenv import os load_dotenv() # take environment variables from .env. api_key = os.getenv("API_KEY") ``` **Node.js (with `dotenv`):** ```javascript require('dotenv').config(); const apiKey = process.env.API_KEY; ``` 4. **Understand the limitations.** `.env` files are stored in plain text. While they are great for local development, they are not a secure solution for production secrets. In production, you should use a vault or your hosting provider's secret management system. **Example (loading secrets from environment variables in Python):** ```python import os api_key = os.getenv("API_KEY") db_password = os.getenv("DB_PASSWORD") ``` ## 9.6 Cookie Security Cookies are a common way to store session information, but they can be vulnerable if not configured correctly. Always set the following attributes on cookies: - **`HttpOnly`:** This prevents the cookie from being accessed by JavaScript, which mitigates the risk of XSS attacks stealing the cookie. - **`Secure`:** This ensures the cookie is only sent over HTTPS. - **`SameSite`:** This attribute helps prevent Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks. - `SameSite=Strict`: The cookie will only be sent on requests originating from the same site. - `SameSite=Lax`: The cookie will be sent on same-site requests and on cross-site top-level navigations (e.g., clicking a link to your site from another site). This is a good balance between security and usability. - `SameSite=None`: The cookie will be sent on all requests, but it **must** be accompanied by the `Secure` attribute. - **`__Host-` and `__Secure-` Prefixes:** - `__Host-`: If a cookie name has this prefix, it must also have `Path=/` and no `Domain` attribute. This locks the cookie to a specific host. - `__Secure-`: If a cookie name has this prefix, it must also have the `Secure` attribute. **Python Example (setting a secure cookie in Flask):** ```python from flask import Flask, make_response app = Flask(__name__) @app.route('/login') def login(): resp = make_response("Setting a secure cookie") resp.set_cookie( 'session_id', 'your_session_id', httponly=True, secure=True, samesite='Lax' ) return resp ```

chapters.title: Performance and Optimization
chapters.content:
# 10. Performance and Optimization

Performance is a feature. This guide provides practical strategies for writing efficient, scalable, and maintainable code. The golden rule is to **measure, don't guess.**

## 10.1 Measure First: Profiling and Benchmarking

Before you optimize, you must measure. Use profiling tools to find the real bottlenecks in your code.

### 10.1.1 Profiling with `cProfile` (Python)

`cProfile` is a built-in Python profiler that can help you understand where your program is spending its time.

**Example:**
```python
import cProfile

def slow_function():
    # ... some slow code ...

cProfile.run('slow_function()')
```
The output will show you how many times each function was called and how much time was spent in each.

### 10.1.2 Benchmarking with `timeit` (Python)

Use benchmarking to compare the performance of different approaches.

**Example:**
```python
import timeit

# Compare list construction methods
timeit.timeit('"-".join(str(n) for n in range(100))', number=10000)
timeit.timeit('"-".join([str(n) for n in range(100)])', number=10000)
```

## 10.2 Algorithmic Efficiency (Big O)

The choice of algorithm has a huge impact on performance. Always consider the time and space complexity of your code.

**Example (O(n^2) vs. O(n)):**
A naive approach to finding duplicates in a list might be O(n^2). A better approach using a hash set is O(n).

```python
# O(n^2) - slow for large lists
def find_duplicates_slow(items):
    for i in range(len(items)):
        for j in range(i + 1, len(items)):
            if items[i] == items[j]:
                return True
    return False

# O(n) - much faster
def find_duplicates_fast(items):
    seen = set()
    for item in items:
        if item in seen:
            return True
        seen.add(item)
    return False
```

## 10.3 Database Optimization

### 10.3.1 Use Indexes

Indexes can dramatically speed up `SELECT` queries. Add indexes to columns that are frequently used in `WHERE` clauses.

**Example (SQL):**
```sql
-- Add an index to the 'email' column of the 'users' table
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email);
```

### 10.3.2 Optimize Queries

Avoid inefficient queries. For example, avoid selecting all columns (`SELECT *`) when you only need a few.

**Bad:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'test@example.com';
```

**Good:**
```sql
SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE email = 'test@example.com';
```

## 10.4 Caching

Cache the results of expensive computations or remote API calls.

### 10.4.1 In-Memory Caching (Python)

Use `functools.lru_cache` for simple in-memory caching.

**Example:**
```python
from functools import lru_cache
import time

@lru_cache(maxsize=128)
def expensive_function(arg):
    time.sleep(2) # Simulate a slow operation
    return f"Result for {arg}"

# The first call will be slow, subsequent calls will be fast
print(expensive_function(1))
print(expensive_function(1))
```

### 10.4.2 Distributed Caching

For larger applications, consider a distributed cache like **Redis** or **Memcached**. This allows multiple instances of your application to share a cache.

## 10.5 Asynchronous Programming

For I/O-bound tasks (e.g., making network requests, reading from a database), asynchronous programming can significantly improve performance.

### 10.5.1 `asyncio` (Python)

**Example:**
```python
import asyncio

async def fetch_data(url):
    print(f"Fetching {url}...")
    await asyncio.sleep(1) # Simulate a network request
    return f"Data from {url}"

async def main():
    urls = ["url1", "url2", "url3"]
    tasks = [fetch_data(url) for url in urls]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())
```

### 10.5.2 `async/await` (JavaScript)

**Example:**
```javascript
async function fetchData(url) {
  console.log(`Fetching ${url}...`);
  await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 1000)); // Simulate a network request
  return `Data from ${url}`;
}

async function main() {
  const urls = ["url1", "url2", "url3"];
  const promises = urls.map(fetchData);
  const results = await Promise.all(promises);
  console.log(results);
}

main();
```

## 10.6 Avoid Premature Optimization

> "Premature optimization is the root of all evil." - Donald Knuth

Start with clear, correct, and simple code. Optimize only when you have identified a real performance bottleneck through profiling.

chapters.title: Infrastructure
chapters.content:
This chapter provides practical guidance for containerized services,
media servers, and virtual machine configuration in data center
projects.

## 11.1 Docker & Containers

- Keep images small and single-purpose. Favor multi-stage builds to
  avoid shipping build tools in runtime images.
- Run processes as non-root inside containers where possible.
- Pin base image versions and scan images for vulnerabilities.
- Use environment variables for configuration and avoid baking secrets
  into images.

### Dockerfile example (multi-stage, small runtime)

```dockerfile
# build stage
FROM node:18-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --production=false
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# runtime stage
FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --production
USER node
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
```

### Using Docker Compose for Local Development

For multi-service applications, `docker-compose` simplifies local development by defining and running all services from a single configuration file.

**`docker-compose.yml` example:**
```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@db:5432/mydb
    depends_on:
      - db
  db:
    image: postgres:14-alpine
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=user
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=password
      - POSTGRES_DB=mydb
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  postgres_data:
```
To start the application, run `docker-compose up`.

## 11.2 Media Servers (transcoding / streaming)

- Right-size resources: media processing is CPU and I/O heavy — allocate
  dedicated nodes or scale horizontally.
- Use hardware acceleration where available (VAAPI, NVENC) for high
  throughput.
- Prefer stateless frontends and persist media files in object storage
  (S3-compatible) or shared storage with strong consistency guarantees.
- Secure media endpoints and validate uploaded content (limits, mime
  types, virus scanning).

## 11.3 Virtual Machines and Orchestration

- Use VM images with minimal surface area and automated provisioning
  (Cloud-init, configuration management).
- For production deployments, prefer orchestration (Kubernetes, Nomad)
  for container orchestration and scaling.
- Define clear resource limits and request settings for containers and
  VMs to avoid noisy-neighbor issues.

## 11.4 Infrastructure as Code (IaC)

Infrastructure as Code is the practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through code instead of manual processes. This improves consistency, reduces errors, and enables automation. Terraform is a popular open-source tool for IaC.

### Terraform Example

This example defines a simple AWS S3 bucket for storing media assets.

**`main.tf`:**
```terraform
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "media_assets" {
  bucket = "cair-media-assets"

  tags = {
    Name        = "CAIR Media Assets"
    Environment = "Production"
  }
}
```
To apply this configuration, run `terraform init` followed by `terraform apply`.

## 11.5 Backups and Recovery

- Regularly back up configuration and critical data with tested restore
  procedures.
- Use immutable artifacts (images, artifacts with checksums) in
  production pipeline.

chapters.title: Documentation Practices
chapters.content:
Good documentation keeps knowledge fresh and reduces onboarding time.
This chapter outlines practical rules for code comments, API docs, and
automated doc generation.

## 12.1 Comment Guidelines

- Explain why, not what. Code should be readable; comments add
  intent and rationale.
- Keep comments brief and up-to-date with code changes.
- Avoid redundant comments that restate obvious code.

## 12.2 Docstrings and API Documentation

- Use language-appropriate docstring formats (Google, NumPy, or
  reStructuredText for Python; Javadoc for Java).
- Document function purpose, parameters, return values, raised
  exceptions, and side effects.

Example (Python Google-style):

```python
def fetch_user(user_id: int) -> dict:
    """Fetch user data by id.

    Args:
        user_id: numeric id of the user.

    Returns:
        A dict containing user fields.

    Raises:
        UserNotFoundError: if the user does not exist.
    """
    pass
```

## 12.3 Auto-generated Documentation

- Use Sphinx (Python), Javadoc (Java), or Doxygen (C/C++) to generate
  API docs.
- Integrate doc generation into CI so docs stay current alongside
  releases.

## 12.4 Documentation for Developers and Users

- Keep a developer README explaining how to build, test, and run the
  project.
- Provide usage examples and quick-start guides for common workflows.
- Maintain a changelog or release notes for notable changes.

## 12.5 Keeping Docs in Sync

- Treat documentation updates as part of code changes and include them
  in pull requests.
- Use templates and checklists for new modules to ensure minimal
  documentation is provided.

## 12.6 Tools and Links

- Sphinx (Python), MkDocs, Jekyll for static site docs.
- Swagger/OpenAPI for API contract documentation.
- Keep doc generation scripts and config files in the repo.

chapters.title: API Documentation
chapters.content:
# 13. API Documentation

Good API documentation is crucial for reducing integration time, preventing misuse, and enabling developers to effectively consume an API. This guide outlines best practices for creating clear, comprehensive, and user-friendly API documentation.

## 13.1 What to Document

A complete API reference should include:

-   **Resource Endpoints:** The URL, HTTP method, and a brief description of each endpoint.
-   **Request/Response Schemas:** Detailed models of the request and response bodies, including data types, constraints, and whether fields are required or optional.
-   **Parameters:** Descriptions of all path, query, and header parameters.
-   **Authentication Requirements:** Clear instructions on how to authenticate with the API.
-   **Status Codes and Error Responses:** A list of all possible HTTP status codes and a standardized error schema.
-   **Code Examples:** Practical examples for common requests and responses in multiple languages (e.g., `curl`, Python, JavaScript).
-   **Versioning Policy:** The API's versioning strategy and rules for backward-compatibility.

## 13.2 Best Tools for API Documentation

Using specialized tools can help generate interactive, machine-readable, and easy-to-maintain documentation.

-   **OpenAPI/Swagger:** The most widely adopted standard for describing REST APIs. An OpenAPI definition file (in YAML or JSON) can be used to generate interactive documentation, client SDKs, and server stubs.
-   **Redoc:** A tool that generates a clean, three-panel documentation website from an OpenAPI definition. It's known for its readability and ease of use.
-   **Postman:** A popular API platform that can create and share collections of API requests. You can write and publish documentation directly within Postman, which is useful for both internal and external consumers.
-   **gRPC:** For RPC systems, API documentation is typically generated from the `.proto` service definitions. Tools like `protoc-gen-doc` can be used to create documentation from these files.

## 13.3 OpenAPI 3.0 Example

An OpenAPI definition is the best way to provide a comprehensive and machine-readable description of your API.

Here is an example of an `openapi.yaml` file for a simple "items" API:

```yaml
openapi: 3.0.0
info:
  title: Simple Items API
  version: 1.0.0
  description: A simple API to manage a list of items.

servers:
  - url: https://api.example.com/v1

paths:
  /items:
    get:
      summary: List all items
      responses:
        '200':
          description: A list of items.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: array
                items:
                  $ref: '#/components/schemas/Item'
    post:
      summary: Create a new item
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/Item'
      responses:
        '21':
          description: The created item.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/Item'

components:
  schemas:
    Item:
      type: object
      properties:
        id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
          readOnly: true
        name:
          type: string
        value:
          type: integer
```

## 13.4 Documenting Authentication

Clearly documenting the authentication process is critical for API adoption.

-   **API Key:** If you use API keys, explain where to get the key and how to send it (e.g., in a header like `X-API-Key`).
-   **OAuth 2.0:** Provide the authorization URL, token URL, and the required scopes. Explain the grant types your API supports (e.g., Authorization Code, Client Credentials).
-   **JWT (JSON Web Tokens):** Explain how to obtain a JWT and how to include it in the `Authorization` header (e.g., `Authorization: Bearer <token>`).

## 13.5 Documenting Error Responses

A standardized error format helps developers handle errors programmatically. Include a section that describes the structure of your error responses.

**Example Error Schema:**

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": "invalid_request",
    "message": "The 'name' field is required.",
    "status": 400
  }
}
```

Common status codes to document include:
- `400 Bad Request`: The request was malformed.
- `401 Unauthorized`: The request requires authentication.
- `403 Forbidden`: The client is not authorized to perform the action.
- `404 Not Found`: The requested resource was not found.
- `500 Internal Server Error`: A generic server error occurred.

## 13.6 Versioning and Deprecation

- Use semantic versioning for APIs where possible (e.g., `/v1/` path).
- Provide deprecation timelines and automated warnings for clients.

## 13.7 Testing and Validation

- Validate API contracts in CI (OpenAPI validation, contract tests).
- Use example-driven tests to ensure docs match implementation.

chapters.title: Code Review Guidelines
chapters.content:
# 14. Code Review Guidelines

Code reviews are a critical part of our development process. They help us maintain a high standard of quality, share knowledge, and foster a collaborative culture. This guide outlines the process, roles, and best practices for effective code reviews.

## 14.1 Roles and Responsibilities

### 14.1.1 The Author

As the author of a pull request (PR), your goal is to make it as easy as possible for reviewers to understand and review your code.

-   **Prepare Your PR:** Create a PR with a clear title and a detailed description. Explain the "what" and the "why" of your changes. Link to the relevant issue or ticket.
-   **Keep it Small:** Keep your PRs small and focused on a single issue. This makes them easier and faster to review.
-   **Self-Review First:** Before you ask for a review, review your own code first. You will often catch simple mistakes yourself.
-   **Respond to Comments:** Respond to every comment, even if it's just to say "done." If you disagree with a suggestion, explain your reasoning respectfully.

### 14.1.2 The Reviewer

As a reviewer, your goal is to provide constructive feedback that improves the quality of the code.

-   **Be Timely:** Try to review PRs in a timely manner. Don't leave authors waiting for days.
-   **Be Thorough:** Review the code for correctness, design, readability, and security. Use the checklist below as a guide.
-   **Be Constructive:** Frame your comments as suggestions, not commands. Ask questions rather than making demands.
-   **Focus on the Code:** Critique the code, not the author.

## 14.2 The Code Review Process

1.  **Create a Pull Request:** The author creates a new PR and assigns it to one or more reviewers.
2.  **Automated Checks:** Automated checks (linters, tests, static analysis) run on the PR. These should pass before a human review begins.
3.  **Human Review:** The reviewer(s) review the code and leave comments.
4.  **Discussion and Iteration:** The author responds to the comments, makes changes, and pushes new commits to the PR.
5.  **Approval:** Once the reviewer(s) are satisfied, they approve the PR.
6.  **Merge:** The author merges the PR.

## 14.3 The Reviewer's Checklist

-   **Correctness:** Does the code do what it's supposed to do? Are there any bugs or edge cases that have been missed?
-   **Design:** Does the code follow the established architecture and design patterns? Is it a good solution to the problem?
-   **Readability and Style:** Is the code clean, readable, and easy to understand? Does it follow our style guide?
-   **Testability:** Is the code testable? Are there enough tests, and are they well-written? Do the tests cover the important cases?
-   **Security:** Does the code introduce any security vulnerabilities? (See the Security Best Practices guide).
-   **Documentation:** Is the code well-documented? Are comments clear and helpful?

## 14.4 The Spirit of Code Review

-   **Assume good intentions.** We are all on the same team, and we all want to build a great product.
-   **Be respectful and professional.**
-   **Provide rationale for your suggestions.** Link to the style guide, a blog post, or a previous decision.
-   **If you disagree, have a conversation.** A code review is a dialogue, not a monologue.

## 14.5 Handling Large Changes

Large changes are difficult to review and are more likely to contain bugs.

-   **Break it Down:** Break large features into smaller, independent PRs that can be reviewed and merged separately.
-   **Design Review First:** For large-scale refactorings or new features, have a design review *before* you start writing code. This will save a lot of time and effort later on.
-   **Feature Flags:** Use feature flags to merge code to the main branch even if the feature is not ready to be released. This avoids long-lived feature branches, which can be a nightmare to merge.

chapters.title: Appendix and References
chapters.content:
## Useful Coding Style Links and Resources

- PEP 8 Python Style Guide: https://pep8.org
- Google Java Style Guide: https://google.github.io/styleguide/javaguide.html
- OWASP Top 10: https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/
- Clean Code Book by Robert C. Martin: https://www.investigatii.md/uploads/resurse/Clean_Code.pdf

## Glossary of Terms

- **Unit Test:** Test of a single component or function
- **Integration Test:** Test of combined components
- **Refactoring:** Improving code structure without changing external behavior
- **Linting:** Automated checking of source code for style issues
- **CI/CD:** Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery/Deployment
